table_content.text: Subprogramme 4. Humanitarian emergency information and advocacy
II.	United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction
A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025***
Overall orientation
Programme of work

text: I.	Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

27.I.1	The mandate is derived from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolution 46/182, in which the Assembly set out the guiding principles of humanitarian response and affirmed the leadership role of the Secretary-General to ensure better preparation and effective response to natural disasters and complex emergencies. This mandate has been confirmed and consolidated through the normative developments of various resolutions over the past 34 years. The Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs is responsible for ensuring the timely, coherent, coordinated and principled response of the international community to natural disasters and complex emergencies and for facilitating the transition from emergency relief to rehabilitation and sustainable development. With over 239 million people in urgent need of humanitarian assistance in 2026 and hunger on the rise, owing to the compounded effects of emerging and prolonged armed conflicts and climate change, ensuring well-coordinated and principled humanitarian assistance by the international community remains as important as ever.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

27.I.2	In 2025, overall humanitarian funding fell to its lowest levels in more than a decade, while nearly 117 million people were forcibly displaced by armed conflict and violence. Armed conflicts continued to take a heavy toll on civilians, protection services ceased in many areas for a vast majority of affected populations, and the impacts of climate change exacerbated humanitarian needs. The global food security crisis affected over 295 million people as acute hunger spread and intensified, marking a sixth consecutive annual increase. In addition, for the first time in this century, two famines unfolded simultaneously.

27.I.3	In the face of these challenges, the Office will continue to enhance the coordination of effective and efficient delivery of humanitarian assistance, together with humanitarian organizations. In line with the humanitarian reset, the Office will work to strengthen a humanitarian system that is locally led and globally supported, driven by communities’ expressed priorities and focused on the most severe needs. By simplifying coordination, reducing duplication and bureaucracy and transitioning to locally led responses, the Office, in collaboration with relevant United Nations entities and other humanitarian organizations, will deliver faster and more accountable humanitarian action.

27.I.4	The Office will continue to develop and promote a common policy on emerging issues and trends with significant humanitarian implications for the United Nations system and its partners; coordinate response to humanitarian emergencies; mobilize United Nations capacity to expedite the provision of humanitarian assistance; and strengthen anticipatory approaches and early warning early action systems, with special attention paid to those most affected by emergency situations and to the need to promote, fund and enable locally led humanitarian action. In addition, the Office will advocate and contribute to better preparedness for more timely humanitarian assistance in emergencies; advocate on humanitarian issues; and strengthen the analysis and availability of timely information on emergencies and natural disasters, in accordance with its mandate. The Office will also support the mobilization of resources for countries and communities facing humanitarian emergencies, including host communities, by scaling up timely and predictable funding, advancing innovative and anticipatory financing mechanisms and strengthening partnerships with development partners and international financial institutions to prevent, reduce and respond to humanitarian suffering. Furthermore, the Office will continue to advocate for safe and unhindered humanitarian access, the safety and security of humanitarian personnel, and respect for international humanitarian law.

27.I.5	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Office will continue to work with national and local governments, affected communities and other stakeholders, including regional disaster management and response organizations, other regional and subregional organizations, private sector actors and first responders, to promote principled, timely and effective humanitarian responses.

27.I.6	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Office will continue, through the Inter-Agency Standing Committee and its subsidiary bodies, to coordinate the development of guidance for the humanitarian system, including on operations policy development and advocacy and supporting implementation of the Secretary-General’s Action Agenda on Internal Displacement. The Office will provide and manage global platforms, such as the United Nations Disaster Assessment and Coordination Team mechanism and the International Search and Rescue Advisory Group, to pool resources and facilitate a coordinated response to humanitarian crises. The Office’s country, regional and liaison offices are strategically located to work with governments and assist in humanitarian preparedness and response. The Office will also collaborate closely with United Nations agencies, funds and programmes, at both the Headquarters and country levels, to streamline the current guidance and indicators associated with thematic humanitarian issues and scale back the frequency and scope of updates to field guides, maps and other reference tools to enable core efforts to focus on contexts where humanitarian needs are greatest and event-driven updates. A humanitarian data collaborative will be established to prevent the siloing of information and encourage humanitarian partners to utilize and contribute to existing platforms rather than building new ones. This will enable effective and consistent system-wide response strategies that meet the humanitarian needs of people in vulnerable situations and minimize duplication and gaps in response efforts.
27.I.7	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	The impact of the global economic contraction, compounded by the impacts of natural disasters and armed conflicts, will continue to be felt and will increase the vulnerability of people;
(b)	Natural disasters will continue to increase in frequency and magnitude, owing, among other things, to the adverse effects of climate change;
(c)	Humanitarian needs and funding requirements will remain high, while humanitarian financing may continue to decline significantly.

27.I.8	The Office integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate, including by promoting the participation and leadership of women in decision-making.

27.I.9	Under the leadership of the Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs, as Chair of the Inter-Agency Standing Committee, the Office will continue to promote and operationalize system-wide commitments on protection from sexual exploitation, abuse and harassment across humanitarian coordination, advocacy and the humanitarian programme cycle. In 2027, the Office will support humanitarian coordinators and humanitarian country teams to sustain inter-agency networks, strengthen accountability frameworks by transitioning standard operating procedures into policy instructions, and reinforce risk management processes to prevent and respond to sexual exploitation, abuse and harassment across the humanitarian system.

27.I.10	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Office will strengthen efforts to ensure that humanitarian programming and response are inclusive of persons with disabilities, with a particular focus on integrating their needs into humanitarian data systems. Building on gaps identified during the previous implementation phase, the Office will develop a comprehensive action plan outlining the institutional and operational adjustments required to ensure disability inclusion.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

27.I.11	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative; in particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026. In addition, and in line with the humanitarian reset, the Office will lead a collaborative diplomacy initiative to address humanitarian needs, piloted in selected contexts, as reflected in the new planned result of subprogramme 2. By negotiating collectively, the United Nations can better defend humanitarian principles, secure humanitarian access and protect civilians. The Office will also establish a humanitarian data collaborative, as outlined in paragraph 27.I.5. Furthermore, coordination will be simplified, including through the merger of clusters and increased use of area-based coordination to ensure humanitarian partners’ focus on cohesive and multisectoral responses. The Office will coordinate with the Department of Operational Support and other relevant United Nations entities to integrate and streamline supply chains through coordinated procurement of key items, pooled use of global freight and logistics, and unbranded in-country services open to the organizations of the United Nations system.

27.I.12	The Office will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals to enable delivery of faster, leaner and more accountable support to people in crises.
Legislative mandates

children.text: Evaluation activities
27.I.15	The following evaluations completed in 2025 have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027:
(a)	Evaluation conducted by the Office on accountability to affected people;
(b)	Inter-Agency Humanitarian Evaluation of the collective response in Somalia;
(c)	Inter-Agency Humanitarian Evaluation of the collective response to the earthquake in Türkiye and the Syrian Arab Republic.

27.I.16	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, the Office will simplify and strengthen coordination mechanisms, ensuring that they more effectively deliver on core functions and accountability commitments. Furthermore, the Office will collaborate with the country teams in advocating for complementarity between short-term humanitarian assistance, adaptation and resilience, including through expanding preparedness, early action and early recovery, as well as longer-term sustainable development focused on the most vulnerable. The Office will also prioritize strengthening humanitarian access, by humanitarian actors, to people with the most severe humanitarian needs and enhancing the participation of affected communities and local organizations in the delivery of humanitarian assistance.
27.I.17	The following evaluations are planned for 2027:
(a)	Evaluation of a topic, to be determined, linked to the Office’s strategic priorities;
(b)	An inter-agency evaluation of a collective humanitarian response or of themes of strategic importance.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Policy and analysis
Objective
27.I.18	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to improve the strategic and operational coherence of humanitarian response.
Strategy
27.I.19	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Identify emerging humanitarian trends to support the comprehensive, authoritative and evidence-based assessment of global humanitarian needs and analyse challenges and opportunities for humanitarian policy and programme development;

(b)	Identify policy and programmatic solutions, best practices and lessons learned to be applied in the humanitarian field and take steps to increase preparedness and operational readiness to respond to humanitarian priorities and reduce humanitarian needs.
27.I.20	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Humanitarian assistance that addresses evolving challenges and reduces the gap between humanitarian needs and response capacity;
(b)	A humanitarian system that remains relevant and adapts to the ever-changing operational environment;
(c)	Humanitarian actors applying innovative solutions in humanitarian operations.
Programme performance in 2025
Locally driven consultations informed humanitarian response priorities, financing and preparedness actions

27.I.21	In 2025, the subprogramme, through the Flagship Initiative, contributed to advancing a more community-centred and locally led humanitarian response as locally driven consultations replaced extractive needs assessments and directly informed response priorities, financing and preparedness actions. In Colombia, community consultations strengthened localization efforts as did a humanitarian financing programme that was fully directed to local and national organizations. In the Philippines, pre-emptive consultations enabled communities and local authorities to shape preparedness and early action, influencing both United Nations programming and municipal approaches. In Somalia, which was not a part of the initial scope of the pilot, lessons from the Flagship Initiative were applied, and locally led consultations informed district-level programming under the Somalia Humanitarian Fund, ensuring the alignment of resources with community-defined priorities.
27.I.22	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 27.I.2).
Table 27.I.2
Performance measure

children.text: Result 3: more efficient and structured humanitarian responses for affected communities – Proposed programme plan for 2027

27.I.27	The humanitarian landscape is evolving in the context of shrinking resources, challenges to humanitarian principles and emerging humanitarian issues, requiring renewed multilateral approaches. Policies guiding humanitarian action must therefore be collectively developed and agreed in order to maximize impact for affected communities.
Lessons learned and planned change

27.I.28	The lesson for the subprogramme was that policy discussions and collective policy development are critical for guiding changes to the humanitarian system. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will focus on policy development for the humanitarian system that ensures principled humanitarian action and genuine localization and strengthens the effectiveness and efficiency of life-saving assistance. The subprogramme will convene policy discussions and generate summaries and recommendations to inform policy development. Emphasis will be placed on engaging a wide array of experts, local leaders and varied participation modalities to ensure that policy dialogue reflects a wide range of operational realities and perspectives.
27.I.29	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 27.I.5).

children.text: a	Reflects updated planned deliverables for 2026. Values in square brackets represent the number of planned deliverables reflected in A/80/6 (Sect. 27).
Subprogramme 2 – Coordination of humanitarian action and emergency response
Objective

27.I.31	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure a coherent, effective and timely humanitarian response to reduce and alleviate human suffering in natural disasters and complex emergencies.
Strategy
27.I.32	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Maintain an effective coordination system to convene humanitarian actors and support senior humanitarian leadership at the global level and in countries affected by natural disasters and complex emergencies, by coordinating timely and objective multisectoral assessments and analyses of humanitarian needs; supporting response planning; and programming and monitoring the provision of assistance to people in need during and in the aftermath of emergencies;

(b)	Promote the delivery of principled humanitarian assistance and coordinate resource mobilization efforts to reduce funding gaps that hamper humanitarian operations, including through effective partnerships with humanitarian actors, Member States, regional organizations and civil society, and the effective management and disbursement of funding from humanitarian pooled funds;

(c)	Increase the use of anticipatory approaches and early warning early action systems to prevent and reduce human suffering, especially with regard to disasters relating to the adverse effects of climate change;

(d)	Strengthen links between humanitarian assistance and efforts to support resilience, early recovery and development to reduce the needs and vulnerabilities of affected people, ensuring that sex-, age- and disability-oriented data are taken into account in all aspects of the response.
27.I.33	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved prioritization of humanitarian responses, based on informed and timely decision-making by humanitarian actors;
(b)	Reduced duplication of response interventions and reduced gaps in humanitarian response;
(c)	An adequately funded international humanitarian system, to respond to increasingly frequent natural disasters and complex emergencies.
Programme performance in 2025
Reaching people in need amid severe funding shortfalls

27.I.34	The subprogramme coordinated the production of the 2025 hyper-prioritized Global Humanitarian Overview in June 2025, on a one-time, exceptional basis and on behalf of the humanitarian community, responding to unprecedented funding shortfalls, to focus reduced resources on saving as many lives as possible. The reprioritization was conducted across each operation on the basis of two main criteria agreed collectively at the global level by the Inter-Agency Standing Committee: (a) concentrating the response where the needs are greatest; and (b) prioritizing life-saving activities, including protection. Following these collective priorities, in 2025, humanitarians delivered at least one form of life-sustaining or life-saving assistance to approximately 98 million people (or 65 per cent of people targeted) through country-specific plans and appeals.
27.I.35	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.I.I).
Figure 27.I.I
Performance measure: number of people receiving at least one form of humanitarian assistance (annual)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: increased participation of local and national actors in humanitarian coordination mechanisms – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

27.I.36	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 93 per cent of countries with local and national actors participating in humanitarian coordination mechanisms, which exceeded the planned target of 86 per cent.
27.I.37	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.I.II).
Figure 27.I.II
Performance measure: percentage of countries with local and national actors participating in humanitarian coordination mechanisms (annual)
Note: Data for 2025 are based on most recent data collected through the annual coordination survey.
Result 2: scaled up anticipatory approaches and early warning early action systems – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

27.I.38	The subprogramme’s work contributed to increased use of anticipatory approaches and early warning early action systems to predict and respond ahead of the humanitarian impact of predictable hazards in 21 countries, which exceeded the planned target of 18.
27.I.39	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.I.III).
Figure 27.I.III
Performance measure: number of countries with increased use of anticipatory approaches and early warning early action systems (annual)
Result 3: strengthened system-wide diplomacy to defend humanitarian principles, secure access and protect civilians – Proposed programme plan for 2027

27.I.40	Humanitarian action is affected by political decisions, conflict dynamics and restrictions on humanitarian access. Diplomatic engagement in support of humanitarian action is a critical tool for defending humanitarian principles and addressing constraints that limit access to affected populations and the protection of civilians. Such engagement, consistent with General Assembly resolution 46/182 and the text contained in the annex thereto, entails engaging with decision-makers, parties to armed conflict and other relevant actors to secure and sustain humanitarian access, promote respect for international humanitarian law and safeguard the operating environment for humanitarian operations. These efforts are undertaken strictly within the Office’s humanitarian coordination mandate. In late 2025, the subprogramme began providing structured support to the principals of United Nations entities under the humanitarian pillar to enhance coordinated diplomatic engagement with Member States and other actors. This support is aimed at ensuring greater coherence, consistency and collective leverage in addressing structural impediments to humanitarian action.
Lessons learned and planned change

27.I.41	The lesson for the subprogramme, amid an increasingly challenging and polarized operating environment, was that collective diplomacy can be more effective in advancing humanitarian goals. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme supported the development of a pilot initiative by the Inter-Agency Standing Committee to strengthen collective diplomatic engagements to influence circumstances and actors restricting humanitarian access in several locations. The subprogramme will reinforce the coordination and support structures to operationalize this system-wide initiative.
27.I.42	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.I.IV).
Figure 27.I.IV
Performance measure: number of joint diplomacy initiatives established to address humanitarian needs (cumulative)

C.	Substantive deliverables

Consultation, advice and advocacy: briefings to the Security Council on specific complex emergency situations; informal consultations with more than 50 Member States on specific emergency situations and the financing of humanitarian requirements; consolidated inter-agency appeals for more than 60 countries and regions affected by humanitarian emergencies; consultations with humanitarian organizations in more than 60 countries on operations, policies and coordination arrangements; and consultations with Member States, regional organizations and the private sector on common humanitarian action.

Subprogramme 3 – Emergency support services
Objective
27.I.44	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to expedite international humanitarian assistance to victims of emergencies and natural disasters.
Strategy
27.I.45	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide effective and well-coordinated operational rapid response support to States affected by disasters and continue to coordinate and support humanitarian actors to prepare for, plan, deliver and monitor the humanitarian response through the humanitarian programme cycle and through the suites of emergency response services, tools and mechanisms;

(b)	Strengthen the capacities of Member State mechanisms for emergency response through the management of various technical response services, including the United Nations Disaster Assessment and Coordination Team mechanism, the International Search and Rescue Advisory Group system, the environmental emergency response mechanism of the Joint Environment Unit of the United Nations Environment Programme and the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, as well as through the provision of training, guidance and technical support, including a continued focus on effective facilitation and functioning of inter-cluster coordination groups and their associated subgroups, and on the role and capacity of local responders;

(c)	Promote collaboration with various partner organizations, including the private sector, through the Connecting Business initiative, which is managed jointly by the Office and the United Nations Development Programme, and United Nations humanitarian civil-military coordination and humanitarian negotiation services; and by continuing to conduct courses and training sessions and meeting regularly with partners through focal points in regional and country offices;

(d)	Standardize and improve operational procedures for international urban search and rescue teams through the International Search and Rescue Advisory Group and activate and coordinate rapid response coordination platforms, such as the virtual On-Site Operations Coordination Centre and the Global Disaster Alert and Response Coordination System, as well as provide near real-time alerts on natural disasters around the world and tools to facilitate response coordination.
27.I.46	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced response capacity of local, regional and international responders for natural disasters;
(b)	More efficient and timely mobilization and deployment of emergency response services;

(c)	Improved effectiveness and prioritization of preparedness and response of the national emergency management authorities in disaster-prone countries, ensuring that assistance and protection reach the people who need it most;
(d)	More effective operational engagement by humanitarian organizations with both local and international private sectors before, during and after emergencies.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened international emergency response through updated guidance

27.I.47	Timely, well-coordinated international assistance is critical to saving lives and reducing suffering in sudden-onset emergencies and depends on common standards, interoperable procedures and predictable coordination arrangements among national authorities and international responders. In 2025, the subprogramme updated three key guidance documents: the United Nations Disaster Assessment and Coordination handbook, the International Search and Rescue Advisory Group guidelines and the United Nations humanitarian civil-military coordination handbook. These updates helped to ensure that Member State response mechanisms, international urban search and rescue teams and humanitarian civil-military coordination actors worked with harmonized procedures that reflect current operational practice, lessons from recent emergencies and the evolving role of local responders and private sector partners in the humanitarian programme cycle.
27.I.48	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 27.I.8).
Table 27.I.8
Performance measure

Enhanced predictability and effectiveness of international response operations, enabling faster mobilization and more coherent support to affected countries during emergency response through updated guidelines

Result 1: enhanced partnership with the private sector in collective humanitarian action – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

27.I.49	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the designation of two additional local private sector networks as members of the Connecting Business initiative in Bangladesh and the Solomon Islands, which did not meet the cumulative planned target of 25 local private sector networks. The target was not met due to a substantial reduction in funding, which necessitated a reduction in the scope of activities under the initiative.
27.I.50	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.I.V).

Result 2: crisis-affected people receive emergency humanitarian assistance in the immediate aftermath of a catastrophe – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

27.I.51	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 522 emergency responders trained in United Nations Disaster Assessment and Coordination system and United Nations humanitarian civil-military coordination to deploy and respond to sudden-onset emergencies, which exceeded the planned target of 370.
27.I.52	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.I.VI).
Figure 27.I.VI

Performance measure: number of emergency responders trained in United Nations Disaster Assessment and Coordination system and United Nations humanitarian civil-military coordination to deploy and respond to sudden-onset emergencies (annual)
Result 3: enhanced global readiness of urban search and rescue teams – Proposed programme plan for 2027

27.I.53	Effective search and rescue operations depend on operational readiness, interoperability and national response capacities to enable faster and safer rescue operations and higher survival rates during the critical first hours following major urban disasters. The International Search and Rescue Advisory Group external classification system is aimed at clarifying the specific capabilities of urban search and rescue teams offering international assistance. By classifying teams according to standardized criteria, countries affected by disasters can better prioritize and integrate international support to strengthen their national response capacity.
Lessons learned and planned change

27.I.54	The lesson for the subprogramme was that early planning, flexible scheduling and leveraging technology can enhance efficiency and ensure greater geographic coverage when conducting classification and reclassification missions. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will conduct International Search and Rescue Advisory Group external classifications and reclassifications for urban search and rescue teams, while strengthening digital coordination and regional collaboration to maintain quality assurance and address resource and travel constraints across the International Search and Rescue Advisory Group network.
27.I.55	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.I.VII).
Figure 27.I.VII
Performance measure: number of urban search and rescue teams classified or reclassified (cumulative)

Subprogramme 4 – Humanitarian emergency information and advocacy
Objective

27.I.57	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure effective advocacy for humanitarian principles and knowledge-sharing in serving populations affected by disasters and emergencies.
Strategy
27.I.58	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Intensify its efforts to raise public awareness of international humanitarian law through targeted communications, clear and accessible messages and proactive media engagement;
(b)	Produce and improve its analytical information products, including digital products and services.
27.I.59	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased respect for international humanitarian law to enhance the protection of affected people and improve unhindered access to humanitarian assistance;
(b)	Improved humanitarian decisions based on more timely and accurate information on humanitarian crises, including intersectoral analysis of the humanitarian situation and its severity and needs.
Programme performance in 2025
Crisis-affected people received data-driven support from humanitarian actors

27.I.60	Each day, new information is produced about humanitarian crises around the world, yet critical warning signs can often go unnoticed. Humanitarians may sense that a crisis is worsening but lack the evidence needed to secure funding or to mobilize resources. Without timely, clear analysis, opportunities for intervention can be missed. To address these challenges, the Centre for Humanitarian Data of the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs developed the Humanitarian Data Exchange: signals, which monitors key humanitarian data sets and generates automated alerts when conditions deteriorate, enhancing resource allocation and response times. In addition to providing timely alerts, Signals offers historical trend analysis delivers crucial context for emerging crises, enabling the United Nations Central Emergency Response Fund to make informed funding decisions. For example, a Signal in January 2025 determined that armed conflict in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo had contributed to the displacement of more than 900,000 people over a period of several weeks. While the Central Emergency Response Fund was already considering an allocation of funding, Signals’ historical trend analysis indicated that was the largest displacement associated with armed conflict in more than five years. As a result, the Fund increased its allocation. Since the launch of Signals in June 2024, other organizations, including the International Rescue Committee and GOALPrime Nigeria have also used it to allocate funds, protect staff and save lives. For example, a Signal flagged severe disaster-driven displacement across Nigeria due to flooding from heavy rains, which enabled GOALPrime Nigeria to quickly plan and design a response and secure funding in record time.
27.I.61	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 27.I.10).
Table 27.I.10
Performance measure

The target was not met due to funding reductions, which significantly reduced editorial output from partners, and in some cases, led to the unavailability of critical information for humanitarian workers.

Result 3: strengthened public awareness of humanitarian crises – Proposed programme plan for 2027

27.I.66	Sustained public awareness of humanitarian crises around the world is critical to mobilizing support for humanitarian assistance. By systematically engaging in advocacy with the public, international organizations can more effectively highlight the importance of issues such as humanitarian needs, delivery of assistance, international humanitarian law, protection of civilians, humanitarian access and funding requirements.
Lessons learned and planned change

27.I.67	The lesson for the subprogramme was that engaging with audiences through both social and national media can help to sensitize local audiences on the need for funding for humanitarian assistance. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will more systematically engage with national audiences using social media and recognized national media channels, including through placements of advocacy materials in print, broadcast and online platforms and engagement of Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs leadership in media interviews and public outreach activities.
27.I.68	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.I.X).

C.	Substantive deliverables

Databases and substantive digital materials: field guides, maps and other reference information for more than 1,000 humanitarian operational partners; and information management, shared data standards for information exchange in the humanitarian community, databases and electronic tools.
D.	Communication deliverables
Outreach programmes, special events and information materials: information materials on coordination of humanitarian action; and World Humanitarian Day campaign on protection of humanitarian workers.

External and media relations: daily media updates to United Nations accredited correspondents through the noon briefing in New York and twice-weekly Palais des Nations briefings in Geneva; at least 12 on-the-record and background media briefings for global media outlets and two dozen interviews to advocate assistance for people caught in humanitarian crises, to reach decision-making audiences in donor countries and countries with humanitarian situations; and comprehensive communications for the launch of the Global Humanitarian Overview (annual consolidated appeals), in support of the Central Emergency Response Fund, and of pledging conferences for large humanitarian crises.

Digital platforms and multimedia content: humanitarian community digital platforms, such as ReliefWeb, ReliefWeb Response and Humanitarian Data Exchange; guidance and templates on various reporting products for humanitarian organizations and Member States; and video, social media and other multimedia content on humanitarian issues, international humanitarian law, international human rights law, humanitarian principles, protection of civilians, specific emergency situations and resource mobilization.

Information and communications technology: global information and communications technology and product support for collaboration services; and information and communications technology for emergency response and business continuity services, business intelligence and data analysis services.

text: II.	United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction

children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

27.II.1	The United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction is the focal point for the coordination of disaster reduction, ensuring synergies among the disaster reduction activities of the United Nations system and regional organizations and activities in socioeconomic development and humanitarian fields. It provides a data-driven and people-centred approach to supporting Governments and stakeholders in identifying, reporting on and reducing existing and emerging disaster risks, with the aim of ensuring that no one is left behind in implementing the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015–2030. The mandate derives from the priorities established in General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolutions 42/169, 54/219, 56/195 and 80/137, as well as the Sendai Declaration and the Sendai Framework adopted by the Assembly in its resolution 69/283.

27.II.2	The General Assembly, in its resolution 69/283, tasked the Office with supporting the implementation, follow-up and review of the Sendai Framework. The Assembly, in its resolution 77/289, adopted the political declaration of the high-level meeting on the midterm review of the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015–2030, which reaffirmed the importance of effective implementation of the Sendai Framework. The Office’s programme plan aligns with the outcomes of the midterm review of the implementation of the Sendai Framework and its Strategic Framework 2026–2030.
Programme of work
Objective

27.II.3	The objective, to which the Office contributes, is to substantially reduce disaster risk and losses in lives, livelihoods and health and in the economic, physical, social, cultural and environmental assets of persons, businesses, communities and countries.
Strategy and external factors for 2027
27.II.4	To contribute to the objective, the Office will:

(a)	Provide guidance to Member States and stakeholders on implementing the Sendai Framework and accelerating the implementation of disaster risk reduction action through the production and dissemination of knowledge resources and best practices and the provision of capacity development and assistance at the regional, national and local levels;

(b)	Support Member States and other development stakeholders in strengthening policy, programmatic and financing coherence between national climate change adaptation and national disaster risk reduction strategies across and within sectors, including by delivering evidence-based advocacy initiatives with clear calls to action to invest in disaster risk reduction and climate change adaptation and raise public awareness on the importance of such efforts;

(c)	Provide support to Member States for reporting on the global targets of the Sendai Framework and for the related indicators of the Sustainable Development Goals and other relevant global frameworks using the Sendai Framework monitoring system;
(d)	Expand the availability, quality and usability of global disaster risk knowledge in order to generate, share and institutionalize actionable insights;

(e)	Promote the systematic institutionalization and resourcing of local risk governance as a core pillar of resilience by supporting the role of subnational actors in driving effective, inclusive and sustained local disaster resilience;

(f)	Support Member States and other stakeholders in embedding disaster risk reduction at the core of public financial management and investment decision-making across both the public and private sectors;
(g)	Lead and support pre-disaster recovery readiness initiatives, including governance, institutional and financing arrangements, for post-disaster response and recovery;

(h)	Co-lead the Early Warnings for All initiative, together with the World Meteorological Organization, and lead on the planning and implementation of the initiative’s pillar 1 focusing on risk knowledge and management, in line with target (g) of the Sendai Framework and General Assembly resolution 77/289;

(i)	Support Member States in strengthening disaster risk reduction policies and programmes in the least developed countries, landlocked developing countries, small island developing States and in humanitarian contexts, through the provision of focused assistance in overcoming the challenges, constraints and gaps that they continue to face, in line with agreed programmes of action;

(j)	Work closely with Member States and stakeholders to follow up on General Assembly resolution 77/289 on the political declaration of the high-level meeting on the midterm review of the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015–2030, with a strengthened focus on the four priority areas identified by Member States, as well as on relevant commitments and decisions contained in the Pact for the Future (Assembly resolution 79/1);

(k)	As co-host of the secretariat for the Santiago network for averting, minimizing and addressing loss and damage associated with the adverse effects of climate change, catalyse technical assistance for developing countries that are particularly vulnerable to the adverse effects of climate change, in line with General Assembly decision 78/546 and resolutions 78/152 and 78/153.
27.II.5	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased accountability for and commitment to implementing the Sendai Framework, including progress towards its goal and targets;

(b)	Improved management and decision-making at the local, national and regional levels regarding current and future disaster risks through an evidence-based understanding of the multi-hazard risk landscape, enabling risk-informed policies, plans, regulatory frameworks and investment that align with the Sendai Framework and the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development;

(c)	A global shift from reactive and more costly post-disaster spending to forward-looking, cost-effective, risk-sensitive investment and the mobilization of greater investment in disaster risk reduction, contributing to higher standards of resilience;
(d)	Resilient recovery that delivers more effective, efficient and timely post-disaster support;
(e)	Increased availability of people-centred, end-to-end multi-hazard early warning systems that reduce disaster impacts;
(f)	Multi-sector and inclusive disaster risk reduction interventions that address the needs of the most vulnerable, thereby contributing to leaving no one behind.
27.II.6	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Disasters will continue to increase in frequency and magnitude owing to, among other underlying factors, the adverse effects of climate change, necessitating the integration of disaster risk reduction, climate action and efforts to build resilience to reduce the impact of disasters;

(b)	Socioeconomic disparities and political tensions have the potential to deepen vulnerabilities of people living in the most disaster-prone countries, necessitating policies, plans, regulatory frameworks and investment decisions based on an accessible and evidence-based understanding of risks;

(c)	The risk of damage and losses from disasters will remain high for the least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States, necessitating an increase in early warning coverage and support to adequately prepare for and respond to disasters.

27.II.7	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, it is recognized in the Sendai Framework that, while States have the overall responsibility for reducing disaster risk, it is a shared responsibility between Governments and relevant stakeholders. Therefore, cooperation with stakeholders, including science and academia, the media, the private sector and civil society, will support an all-of-society approach to disaster risk reduction. The Office will support private sector coordination efforts with national and local governments and strengthening micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises’ resilience to disasters. The Office will continue to work through the Sendai Stakeholder Engagement Mechanism and other region-specific mechanisms to promote inclusive and coherent implementation of the Sendai Framework and risk-informed development that leaves no one behind. The Office will also emphasize cooperation at the local level by promoting disaster resilience-building in cities.

27.II.8	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, as Chair of the Senior Leadership Group on Disaster Risk Reduction for Resilience and as co-lead of the Early Warnings for All initiative, the Office will promote synergies within the United Nations system on interventions relating to disaster risk reduction and multi-hazard early warning systems. The Office will continue leading and coordinating the efforts of more than 40 United Nations entities engaged in the implementation of the United Nations Plan of Action on Disaster Risk Reduction for Resilience, further strengthening system-wide coherence and risk-informed approaches.

27.II.9	The Office integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. In line with the Sendai Framework, the Office will work to promote the full, equal and effective participation and leadership of women in the design, management, resourcing and implementation of gender-sensitive disaster risk reduction policies, plans and programmes, and enable Governments to better incorporate a gender perspective into national risk reduction strategies, early warning systems, climate adaptation and resilience-building.

27.II.10	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, and recognizing that disasters and the consequent disruption to physical, social, economic and environmental networks and support systems disproportionately affect persons living with disabilities and their families, the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction will increase advocacy on disability-inclusive disaster risk reduction and ensure the participation of persons with disability stakeholder groups in processes that set the global and regional disaster risk reduction agendas, with the active involvement of its regional offices. The Office will support States Parties’ efforts to implement the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities. Emphasis will be placed on supporting Member States in ensuring that persons with disabilities and their needs are included in the implementation of the Sendai Framework and its expected results, goal and targets, including through the availability of disaggregated data.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

27.II.11	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects efforts of the programme to implement all mandates in a more efficient and effective manner and contains no substantive adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative.

27.II.12	The Office will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.
Evaluation activities
27.II.13	An evaluation of the “Making Cities Resilient 2030” initiative, conducted by the Office in 2025, has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

27.II.14	In response to the preliminary results of the evaluation referenced above, the Office will strengthen its support for locally led action on disaster risk reduction by sharpening the strategic positioning, governance arrangements and partnership model of the “Making Resilient Cities 2030” initiative. The Office will prioritize actions that enhance national-local linkages, activate peer learning through resilience hubs and improve resource mobilization and financial sustainability to accelerate implementation in the final phase of the Sendai Framework.

27.II.15	An evaluation of the effectiveness of the Office’s support to Member States on their implementation of the Sendai Framework, with specific focus areas to be determined, is planned to be conducted by the Office in 2027.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States and stakeholders review progress on the implementation of the Sendai Framework and identify recommendations to enhance disaster risk reduction

27.II.16	United Nations General Assembly resolution 72/218 and the recurrent Assembly resolutions on disaster risk reduction recognize the Global Platform for Disaster Risk Reduction as the global multi-stakeholder forum for review of progress on the implementation of the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction. The Office organized and convened the eighth session of the Global Platform, which was held in Geneva from 2 to 6 June 2025. The Global Platform convened policymakers, practitioners and stakeholders to share successes and lessons, address common challenges and adopt innovations to accelerate the implementation of the Sendai Framework in its final five years. In line with its overarching theme, “Every day counts, act for resilience today,” the Global Platform showcased the role of emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence and enhanced risk information tools, in supporting faster, risk‑informed action, as well as the wider use of data on climate change and early warning systems to strengthen anticipatory action.
27.II.17	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 27.II.1).
Table 27.II.1
Performance measure

table_content.text: 2023 (actual)
2024 (actual)
2025 (actual)

The Co-Chairs’ summary of the Global Platform, entitled “Geneva call for disaster risk reduction”, reaffirmed the high-level commitment to accelerating implementation of the Sendai Framework, calling for better data to understand risk, enhanced use of technology to leapfrog progress, more integrated risk governance and cooperation, more risk-informed investment, scaling-up of early warning systems, leave no one behind approaches and better preparedness to “build back better”

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: strengthened inclusive multi-hazard early warning systems that reach the most vulnerable – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

27.II.18	The programme’s work contributed to 26 small island developing States, least developed countries and landlocked developing countries having accessible, understandable, usable and relevant disaster risk information and assessment available to people at the national and local levels, based on national reporting against target G5 of the Sendai Framework monitor, which did not meet the planned target of 30. However, the Office estimates, based on its technical support, that 32 additional small island developing States, least developed countries and landlocked developing countries have accessible, understandable, usable and relevant disaster risk information and assessment available to people at the national and local levels, for a total of 58 countries. These countries have not yet reported officially against target G5 in the Sendai Framework monitor, and thus the planned target was not achieved in accordance with the defined reporting approach.
27.II.19	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.II.I).
Figure 27.II.I

Performance measure: number of small island developing States, least developed countries and landlocked developing countries that have accessible, understandable, usable and relevant disaster risk information and assessment available to the people at the national and local levels (cumulative)
Note: Data are based on national reporting against target G5 of the Sendai Framework monitor.
Result 2: national strategies in place to finance disaster risk reduction – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

27.II.20	The programme’s work contributed to 11 countries with disaster risk reduction financing strategies or integrated national financing frameworks with a disaster risk reduction lens, which exceeded the planned target of 10.
27.II.21	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.II.II).
Figure 27.II.II
Performance measure: number of countries with disaster risk reduction financing strategies or integrated national financing frameworks with a disaster risk reduction lens (cumulative)
Result 3: Governments assess, strengthen and institutionalize their readiness for resilient recovery to build back better – Proposed programme plan for 2027

27.II.22	Recovery readiness enables governments to plan and implement recovery processes that are more timely, inclusive and effective, reducing the long-term impact of disaster losses. To support recovery readiness, the Office developed the recovery readiness assessment framework to help governments in identifying gaps and prioritizing improvements before disasters strike.
Lessons learned and planned change

27.II.23	The lesson for the Office was that countries would benefit most through targeted technical support to operationalize the recovery readiness assessment framework, translating the assessment into actionable priorities, sequencing and institutional reforms. In applying the lesson, the Office will roll out the recovery readiness assessment framework, in partnership with development, humanitarian, emergency response and other key national actors, promoting a shift from reactive recovery planning to proactive, all-of-government and all-of-society readiness. The Office will support countries in identifying gaps, prioritizing improvements and developing readiness strategies to institutionalize policies, coordination, resourcing and capacities before disaster strikes.
27.II.24	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 27.II.III).
Figure 27.II.III
Performance measure: number of governments that have generated policy insights on their recovery systems based on applying the recovery readiness assessment framework (cumulative)
